executive.explanation_en: a person who has an important job in a company
executive.example_sentence: The bank executive decided to hire more staff.
executive.original_sentence: A young bank executive related this personal experience about work capacity.

short notice.explanation_en: not enough time to prepare for something before it happens
short notice.example_sentence: I had to pack my bags at short notice for a business trip.
short notice.original_sentence: One of the other executives in our bank left us with very short notice.

on the spot.explanation_en: in a difficult situation where you have to make a decision or act immediately
on the spot.example_sentence: The boss put me on the spot by asking me to explain the mistake in front of everyone.
on the spot.original_sentence: This put our department on the spot.

postponed.explanation_en: to arrange for something to happen at a later time
postponed.example_sentence: The meeting was postponed until next week.
postponed.original_sentence: The fellow leaving had filled an important job, and his work couldn't be postponed or left undone.

up to his neck.explanation_en: to be very busy or have too much of something to deal with
up to his neck.example_sentence: I can't go out tonight; I'm up to my neck in paperwork.
up to his neck.original_sentence: Neither of them flatly refused, said the vice president, but each stated that he is up to his neck now with his own pressing work.

pressing.explanation_en: needing to be dealt with immediately
pressing.example_sentence: There is a pressing need for more volunteers.
pressing.original_sentence: Neither of them flatly refused, said the vice president, but each stated that he is up to his neck now with his own pressing work.

overload.example_sentence: The system crashed because of an information overload.
overload.original_sentence: I'm wondering if you could handle some of the overload temporarily?

turn down.explanation_en: to refuse or reject an offer or request
turn down.example_sentence: It is hard to turn down such a good job offer.
turn down.original_sentence: Throughout my working career, I've learned that it never pays to turn down what looks like an opportunity.

vacated.example_sentence: She was asked to fill the vacated position immediately.
vacated.original_sentence: So I agreed and promised to do my very best to handle all the vacated job as well as keep up with my own work.

take on.explanation_en: to accept a new responsibility or task
take on.example_sentence: I decided to take on extra classes to improve my skills.
take on.original_sentence: I walked out of his office knowing I had taken on a big job.

wiggled out of.explanation_en: to avoid doing something that you should do, often in a clever or dishonest way
wiggled out of.example_sentence: He tried to wiggle out of his chores by saying he was sick.
wiggled out of.original_sentence: I was just as busy as the two others in my department who had wiggled out of this extra duty.

figure out.explanation_en: to understand or solve a problem
figure out.example_sentence: I need to figure out how to fix this computer.
figure out.original_sentence: I sat down to figure out how I could increase my personal efficiency.

efficiency.explanation_en: the ability to do things well without wasting time or energy
efficiency.example_sentence: We need to improve the efficiency of our office processes.
efficiency.original_sentence: I sat down to figure out how I could increase my personal efficiency.

routine.explanation_en: done as part of a regular procedure rather than for a special reason
routine.example_sentence: Checking emails is part of my daily routine.
routine.original_sentence: I came up with some good ones, like working out an arrangement with my secretary to channel all routine telephone calls to me during a certain hour each day.

creep in.explanation_en: to start to happen or exist slowly and without being noticed
creep in.explanation_zh: 悄悄潜入，不知不觉产生
creep in.example_sentence: Errors began to creep in as we got tired.
creep in.original_sentence: I was amazed to discover how much inefficiency I had let creep in.

strain.explanation_en: a feeling of pressure or worry caused by having too much to do
strain.example_sentence: The work was hard, but I did it with no strain.
strain.original_sentence: Within a week's time, I was dictating twice as many letters, handling fifty percent more phone calls, attending half again as many meetings, all with no strain.

complimented.explanation_en: to tell someone that you admire or approve of something they have done
complimented.example_sentence: The teacher complimented her on her excellent essay.
complimented.original_sentence: The vice president called me in. He complimented me on doing a fine job.

confessed.explanation_en: to admit that you have done something or that something is true
confessed.example_sentence: He confessed that he had forgotten the meeting.
confessed.original_sentence: Then he confessed that he had already cleared with the bank's executive committee.

authorized.explanation_en: to give official permission for something
authorized.example_sentence: The manager authorized the extra payment.
authorized.original_sentence: They had authorized him to combine the two jobs, put them both in my charge.

substantial.explanation_en: large in amount, value, or importance
substantial.example_sentence: She received a substantial pay raise after her promotion.
substantial.original_sentence: And give me a substantial increase in salary.
